reasoning: The resolution criteria require the CDC to report at least one case of human-to-human H5N1 spread globally between July 17, 2024, and December 31, 2025. As of early June 2025—roughly 10.5 months into the forecasting window with about 6.5 months remaining—there have been no reported cases of human-to-human transmission. Recent US human cases (totaling ~70 cases primarily from dairy and poultry outbreaks, as well as an extensive investigation in Missouri) have all tested negative for evidence of human-to-human spread. The CDC explicitly confirms no person-to-person spread has been detected. There have also been no global clusters reported so far in 2024-2025. While limited, non-sustained person-to-person transmission occurred historically (e.g., 2004, 2006, 2007), it has not happened in over a decade. The current baseline risk is slightly elevated due to widespread circulation in mammals (notably US cattle), but the lack of evolving transmissibility to date shrinks the likelihood considerably. Given that no event has materialized, the time remaining makes the probability slightly lower than the community consensus of 10% from late May, so I estimate 6%.
